mappings.rationaleAI: Directly covers scarcity, division of labour, opportunity cost, economies of scale, and economic development (A.1-A.8).
Analyzes the behaviour of individuals, households, and firms, plus competition, monopoly, and market structures (B.1).
Extends basic economic concepts by showing how Kahneman and Tversky's prospect theory and cognitive biases cause actual decisions to depart from rational-choice models (A.1, B.1).
Formalizes strategic interaction -- Nash equilibria, prisoner's dilemmas -- underlying competition and comparative advantage (A.6, A.7).
Examines utility, marginal utility, and rational choice among scarce resources, directly relevant to marginality and optimization (A.1, A.3, A.4).
Presents Smith's foundational ideas on the division of labour, competition, free trade, and the invisible hand as organizing principles of market economies (A.2, A.6, A.7).
Explains Keynesian macroeconomics -- aggregate demand, the multiplier, and the case for fiscal intervention -- and the mixed economy model (B.3, C.1.c).
Presents Marx's economic theory including surplus value, capital accumulation, and alienation, forming the critique underlying the socialist model (A.5, C.1.b).
Examines the private enterprise economy, capital formation, market forces, and industrialization as the dynamics of capitalist systems (C.1.a, C.2).
Analyzes centrally planned economies and Soviet-type economic systems, from Gosplan allocation to command pricing (C.1.b, C.3).
Explores the socialist economic model and social democracy as alternatives to pure capitalism, from cooperative ownership to welfare states (C.1.b, C.1.c).
Provides historical context for the centrally planned economy, from Lenin's New Economic Policy through Stalinist industrialization to perestroika (C.3).
Serves as a case study of transition from centrally planned to mixed economy, illustrating the challenges of systemic economic transformation (C.3, C.1.c).
Addresses economic growth and development concepts, GDP measurement, and mixed economies in developing countries (A.8, C.4).
Traces the historical comparison of different economic systems and their divergent growth trajectories across countries (C).
Examines the concept of competition, competitive markets, and government intervention to prevent monopoly power in free market economies (A.6, C.1.a).
Introduces Malthus's principle of population and the law of diminishing returns, foundational to classical political economy and growth theory (A.3, A.8).
Illustrates division of labour, capital formation, and the transition to industrial economic systems in 18th- and 19th-century Britain (A.2, A.5, C.5.a).
